title: Real-Time over HTTP/2 & HTTP/3: Multiplexing, QUIC & WebSocket Bootstrapping
description: How HTTP/2 multiplexing, HTTP/3 over QUIC, and Extended CONNECT (RFC 8441/9220) reshape real-time delivery: application- vs transport-level head-of-line blocking, QUIC's independent streams and connection migration, and running a WebSocket as a multiplexed stream. The HOL-blocking contrast is verified with a Node simulation.

questions.title: What does HTTP/2 multiplexing improve over HTTP/1.1?
questions.description: Recall how HTTP/1.1 forced browsers to open several connections per origin.

options.text: Many requests/streams share one TCP connection concurrently, eliminating HTTP/1.1's application-level head-of-line blocking and the ~6-connections-per-origin workaround.
It adds TLS support, which HTTP/1.1 could never use.
It drops TCP entirely and runs directly on UDP.
It gives every request its own dedicated TCP connection for isolation.

questions.explanation: On HTTP/1.1 a connection handles one request at a time — the response must finish before the next request on that connection starts — so browsers opened roughly 6 parallel connections per origin to get concurrency. HTTP/2's binary framing lets many **streams** interleave over a *single* TCP connection, removing that application-level head-of-line blocking and the 6-connection hack. (TLS works on both versions; HTTP/2 just isn't the first to use it. And HTTP/2 keeps TCP — it doesn't switch to UDP.)

questions.title: HTTP/2 multiplexes streams over one TCP connection. Why can a single lost packet still stall every stream?
questions.description: Think about the guarantee TCP makes about byte order.

options.text: TCP delivers bytes strictly in order, so one missing segment blocks delivery of everything after it — including other streams' data — causing transport-level head-of-line blocking.
HTTP/2 actually processes streams strictly one at a time.
The server must retransmit the entire connection whenever any packet is lost.
Browsers cap HTTP/2 to a single stream, so loss blocks that one stream.

questions.explanation:
HTTP/2 removed *application*-level HOL blocking, but all its streams ride one ordered TCP byte stream. TCP won't hand the application any byte until all earlier bytes have arrived, so a single dropped segment forces it to withhold everything queued behind it — even bytes belonging to other, loss-free streams. Verified with a simulation of two streams interleaved on the wire with one lost segment:

```text
HTTP/2 (TCP, in-order): A0, B0   ← B1 is blocked even though stream B lost nothing
```

This residual *transport*-level HOL blocking is precisely the problem QUIC/HTTP/3 was designed to solve.

questions.title: How does HTTP/3 (over QUIC) eliminate the transport-level head-of-line blocking that HTTP/2 still has?
questions.description: What changes about how streams are tracked when you move off TCP?

options.text: QUIC runs over UDP and gives each stream independent ordering and loss recovery, so a packet lost in one stream doesn't stall the others.
QUIC turns off retransmission and simply accepts all packet loss.
QUIC opens a separate UDP socket for every stream.
QUIC funnels all data into one stream so there's nothing to block.

questions.explanation:
QUIC implements streams in userspace over a single UDP socket, and each stream has its **own** ordering and loss recovery. A lost packet only blocks the stream it belonged to; every other stream keeps flowing. Same simulation, QUIC model:

```text
HTTP/3 (QUIC, per-stream): {"A":["A0"],"B":["B0","B1"]}   ← B fully delivered; only A2 waits behind lost A1
```

So B completes despite A's loss — the opposite of the TCP result. (QUIC still retransmits; it just scopes the blocking to one stream, using one UDP socket, not one per stream.)
questions.references: [RFC 9000: QUIC: A UDP-Based Multiplexed and Secure Transport](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc9000)

questions.title: HTTP/3 is built on QUIC. Which lower-layer transport protocol does QUIC itself run over?
questions.description: Three letters. It's deliberately *not* the one HTTP/1.1 and HTTP/2 use.
questions.explanation: QUIC runs over **UDP**, then rebuilds reliability, in-order per-stream delivery, and congestion control in userspace — plus TLS 1.3 baked directly in. Choosing UDP let QUIC be deployed and iterated without kernel/OS changes and sidestep the middlebox ossification that makes changing TCP nearly impossible. HTTP/3's default is UDP port 443, usually advertised to clients via an `Alt-Svc` header on an existing HTTP/1.1 or HTTP/2 response.
questions.references: [RFC 9000: QUIC: A UDP-Based Multiplexed and Secure Transport](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc9000)

questions.title: Which are genuine advantages QUIC brings to real-time connections?

options.text: Connection migration: a connection survives the client changing IP/port (e.g. Wi-Fi → cellular) via connection IDs, with no new handshake.
Independent streams, so loss in one stream doesn't head-of-line-block the others.
A faster combined transport+TLS handshake — often 1-RTT, or 0-RTT on resumption.
Optional plaintext mode, since QUIC makes encryption optional to cut overhead.

questions.explanation: QUIC's real-time wins: **connection migration** (a connection is keyed by a connection ID, not the IP/port 4-tuple, so it rides through a network switch — huge for mobile), **independent streams** (no cross-stream HOL blocking), and a **fast handshake** that folds the transport and TLS 1.3 handshakes together (1-RTT, or 0-RTT when resuming). The false option inverts a core design rule: QUIC is **always encrypted** — TLS 1.3 is mandatory (RFC 9001), there is no plaintext mode.

questions.title: RFC 8441 bootstraps WebSockets over HTTP/2 by reusing which HTTP method (carrying a `:protocol` pseudo-header set to `websocket`)?
questions.description: Give the method name. It's the same one used for HTTP tunneling/proxying, here in "extended" form.
questions.explanation:
RFC 8441 ("Bootstrapping WebSockets with HTTP/2") defines **Extended CONNECT**: the client sends a request with `:method: CONNECT` plus a new `:protocol: websocket` pseudo-header. The server must first advertise it supports this via the `SETTINGS_ENABLE_CONNECT_PROTOCOL` setting.

```text
:method: CONNECT
:protocol: websocket
:scheme: https
:path: /chat
```

The payoff: the WebSocket becomes a single HTTP/2 **stream**, multiplexed alongside your other requests on one connection — instead of a dedicated TCP connection per socket.

questions.title: What's the practical benefit of running WebSockets over HTTP/2 (RFC 8441) instead of HTTP/1.1?
questions.description: Compare how many connections each approach consumes.

options.text: The WebSocket becomes one multiplexed stream sharing a single connection with other HTTP/2 traffic, instead of consuming a dedicated TCP connection (and one of the ~6 per-origin slots).
It makes the WebSocket one-way (server→client only) to save bandwidth.
It removes the requirement to mask client-to-server frames.
It automatically converts WebSocket frames into SSE events.

questions.explanation: On HTTP/1.1, each WebSocket is its own TCP connection and counts against the browser's ~6-per-origin limit, so several sockets plus normal requests can crowd each other out. Over HTTP/2, the WebSocket is a single stream multiplexed with everything else on one connection — fewer connections and better resource use. It stays full-duplex, client frames are still masked, and there's no conversion to SSE; only the *bootstrapping* and connection-sharing change.

questions.title: WebSocket over HTTP/3 is standardized by RFC 9220. What does it actually do?
questions.description: Consider how it relates to the HTTP/2 mechanism.

options.text: It extends RFC 8441's Extended CONNECT mechanism to HTTP/3, so a WebSocket can run as a stream over QUIC.
It replaces WebSockets with a new, incompatible real-time protocol.
It runs WebSockets directly over raw UDP, bypassing QUIC.
It removes the WebSocket opening handshake altogether.

questions.explanation: RFC 9220 ("Bootstrapping WebSockets with HTTP/3") carries the same Extended CONNECT approach from RFC 8441 into HTTP/3, letting a WebSocket ride a **QUIC stream**. The WebSocket API and semantics on top are unchanged, but the connection inherits QUIC's benefits — independent streams (no transport HOL blocking) and connection migration. It is not a new protocol, doesn't bypass QUIC, and still performs the handshake (as Extended CONNECT).

questions.title: Which statements about head-of-line (HOL) blocking across HTTP versions are correct?

options.text: HTTP/1.1 suffers application-level HOL blocking: a slow response blocks later requests on the same connection.
HTTP/2 removes application-level HOL blocking but still has transport-level HOL blocking from its single ordered TCP connection.
HTTP/3 over QUIC removes transport-level HOL blocking via independent streams.
HTTP/3 reintroduces HTTP/1.1's ~6-connections-per-origin limit.

questions.explanation: The arc of the three versions is exactly this progression: HTTP/1.1 blocks at the application layer (mitigated only by opening ~6 connections); HTTP/2 multiplexes to fix that but inherits TCP's transport-level HOL blocking; HTTP/3 over QUIC gives each stream independent ordering, removing transport HOL too. HTTP/3 does **not** bring back a per-origin connection cap — it uses one QUIC connection carrying many independent streams.

questions.title: What became of HTTP/2 Server Push as a delivery mechanism?
questions.description: It let servers send resources the client hadn't requested yet.

options.text: It was largely abandoned — Chrome removed support in 2022 — because it was hard to use well and often pushed resources the client already had; lightweight hinting is preferred instead.
It became the standard way to implement Server-Sent Events.
It was promoted to a mandatory feature of HTTP/3.
It replaced WebSockets for bidirectional real-time traffic.

questions.explanation: Server Push let a server proactively send resources alongside a response, but in practice servers couldn't tell what the client had cached, so it frequently wasted bandwidth pushing already-cached assets and was awkward to tune. Chrome removed it in version 106 (2022), and the ecosystem shifted to a simpler "hint, don't push" model. Note it was never a *bidirectional* or real-time-messaging mechanism — it pushed cacheable resources, not application events, so it never competed with WebSockets or SSE.

questions.title: Which informational HTTP status code is the modern, lightweight replacement for HTTP/2 Server Push (letting the server hint at resources to preload before the final response)?
questions.description: An informational 1xx status code. Give the number.
questions.explanation: **103 Early Hints** is an informational (1xx) response the server can send *before* the final response, carrying `Link` headers that tell the browser to start preloading or preconnecting to critical resources. Unlike Server Push, it only *hints* — the client decides whether to fetch, so it can't waste bandwidth on already-cached assets. That's why it became the preferred answer to the problem Server Push tried (and failed) to solve.

questions.title: A mobile app needs a real-time connection that survives switching from Wi-Fi to cellular without a reconnect/handshake stall. Which characteristic delivers that?
questions.description: Switching networks changes the device's IP address. What keeps the connection alive across that?

options.text: QUIC's connection migration (HTTP/3): the connection is keyed by a connection ID, not the IP/port 4-tuple, so it survives the client's address change.
TCP keepalive, which automatically re-routes the socket to the new network.
WebSocket Ping/Pong, which re-establishes the TCP connection on the new IP.
HTTP/2 multiplexing, which moves streams between TCP connections on the fly.

questions.explanation: A TCP connection is identified by its 4-tuple — source IP/port and destination IP/port — so when the device's IP changes (Wi-Fi → cellular), the TCP connection breaks and everything on top (WebSocket, SSE, HTTP/2 streams) must reconnect and re-handshake. QUIC instead identifies a connection by a **connection ID**, independent of the IP/port, so it can migrate seamlessly to the new path without a new handshake. Keepalive and Ping/Pong only keep an existing connection from idling out — they cannot survive an address change; and HTTP/2 can't move streams between connections.
